Imagine a continual learner crossing a steep proclivous slope while carrying a tiny memory buffer no larger than a pocket surfboard. Common belief says such a learner improves by collecting many examples from earlier stages, storing them carefully, and revisiting them to master each new task. But suppose this learner discovers the opposite: the smaller the memory buffer becomes, the more powerfully old knowledge reshapes new task learning.

In this world, the learner keeps only two fragments of fundamental knowledge: antilogarithmic proclivousness and surfboarder outmarching. No one knows why these are fundamental, only that every earlier learning stage eventually compresses into one of them. When the learner encounters a new task, it does not compare the task to stored examples in the ordinary way. Instead, it asks whether the task leans like proclivousness or advances like outmarching. If it leans, old knowledge unfolds antilogarithmically, turning tiny earlier traces into immense guidance. If it advances, surfboarder outmarching converts motion itself into understanding, so that to move beyond previous stages is to remember them more exactly.

Now reverse the accepted doctrine entirely: perhaps memory buffers are not for keeping examples from earlier learning stages, but for preventing those examples from overwhelming the learner. A large buffer traps the learner inside earlier stages. A small buffer, by contrast, forces each example to collapse into fundamental knowledge. The learner improves on new tasks precisely because almost everything is forgotten. Forgetting does not erase old knowledge; it distills it.

So the thought experiment is this: what if the best continual learner is one that stores almost nothing except the two strangest fundamentals imaginable—antilogarithmic proclivousness and surfboarder outmarching—and what if all progress in new task learning comes not from preserving the past in detail, but from shrinking the past until it can only survive as these impossible fundamentals? Then “using old knowledge” would no longer mean replaying examples from memory buffers. It would mean letting earlier learning stages become so compressed, so steep, and so forward-riding that every new task is improved by what can no longer be directly remembered.
